stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 22nd.
stocks.news:
Markets ripped higher on tech strength. Elon Musk's SpaceX just got the option to buy AI coding startup Cursor for $60B later this year. If SpaceX walks away, it still owes Cursor $10B for work already done. Microsoft (MSFT +2.07%) reportedly was looking to scoop Cursor before SpaceX beat them to it.

Tesla (TSLA +0.28%) and Rivian (RIVN +3.44%) had opposing charges. Rivian started R2 SUV production just 5 days after one of its factories got hit by a tornado. Tesla beat on earnings but missed on deliveries. Both stocks are down roughly 10% YTD.

Spirit Airlines is fighting its second bankruptcy in a year. The Trump administration is now close to a $500M rescue that could give the U.S. government up to 90% ownership post-bankruptcy. A government-owned airline would be an unusual American thing.

economy.note: US 10-Year and Gold as of 4:00 PM ET April 22nd; WTI Crude reflects today's intraday move via investing.com.
economy.commentary:
Gulf countries are running out of US dollars thanks to Hormuz closures. A closed Strait means fewer oil sales and fewer dollars recycling back. Some Gulf states are asking Treasury for emergency dollar supply; others are hinting they may turn to the Chinese yuan if dollars run dry. Watch this quietly. It's a crack in the petrodollar.

San Francisco just became the first U.S. city ever to hit $8/gallon diesel. Three culprits: the Iran war (global oil up), California's clean-fuel blend (tight supply), and the highest fuel taxes in the country. Diesel moves trucks and trains, so $8 fuel means higher grocery and restaurant prices are coming.

Oil spiked near $100 on Brent after Iran seized two container ships in the Strait of Hormuz Wednesday. This was hours after Trump extended the ceasefire indefinitely. The White House says seizing non-U.S. ships doesn't violate the ceasefire. Technically fine, narratively not great.

crypto.note: Numbers as of 4:00 PM ET on April 22nd.
crypto.commentary:
DoorDash (DASH -0.09%) is rolling out stablecoin payments to Dashers and merchants in 40+ countries via Stripe's Tempo blockchain. Faster payouts, cheaper cross-border, fewer steps. Mastercard, Visa, UBS, and Klarna already use the same rails. Another stablecoin-into-mainstream moment.

Crypto billionaire Justin Sun filed suit against World Liberty Financial, the Trump family-backed crypto project. Core claim: WLF added a hidden "blacklisting" function in August 2025 that froze Sun's 2.9 billion WLFI tokens. Those were once worth over $1B. Today? About $75M.

real_estate.homebuilder: Rates just ticked to a four-week low. MND shows the 30-year at 6.32% and Freddie Mac's weekly average at 6.37% (versus 6.83% a year ago). The tentative US-Iran ceasefire pushed oil lower earlier, which dragged rates with it. Careful though. With Iran seizing ships in Hormuz yesterday and oil back near $100, the ceasefire's fragility could snap rates right back up.
real_estate.commentary:
Total active inventory across the 15-city board is 2,726, up from 2,691 yesterday. Fresh supply today came in at 193 new listings, lighter than yesterday's 219. Total is growing but the daily add is thinning. Longer days on market are starting to show up, which is why inspections and prep matter more now.

📍 Alameda & Contra Costa Snapshot (04/23/26)

📍 Fremont: 24 new. Steady flow, anchor of Alameda County inventory.

📍 Union City: 8 new. Slight uptick from yesterday's 7.

📍 Newark: 3 new. Light stock.

📍 Hayward: 22 new. Leading the I-880 corridor for the third day running.

📍 Milpitas: 3 new. Pending 29 townhomes still loaded.

📍 Pleasanton: 14 new. Tri-Valley steady.

📍 Dublin: 9 new. Cooler today.

📍 San Ramon: 19 new. Balanced.

📍 Livermore: 13 new. East Tri-Valley picking back up.

📍 Castro Valley: 7 new. Quiet.

📍 Danville: 19 new. Strongest Contra Costa entry.

📍 San Leandro: 13 new. Consistent.

📍 San Lorenzo: 3 new. Minimal.

📍 Oakland: 36 new. Volume leader, softer than yesterday's 50.

Home inspections are back. During the pandemic, buyers waived inspections to win bids. That trend is fading fast. Buyers are asking for inspections again, deals are taking longer, home warranties are making a comeback, and thermal imaging is becoming standard. For our listing agents, this is the playbook shift: pre-listing inspection isn't optional anymore. If your seller skips it, a buyer's inspector will find something in week 3 and you'll renegotiate from a weaker seat. Get ahead of it.

For listing agents: Rates hit a four-week low. Buyers who've been watching from the sidelines have a reason to come off the fence. Price at the last comp, pre-inspect the home, and push for offers in the first 10 days. The window for "rates might drop more" is still open but it only takes one Hormuz headline to slam it shut.
